perturbation.original_text: Company shall have Seven (7) business days after its receipt to accept (or reject for a legitimate business reason) any order submitted by Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall have One (1) business day after its receipt to accept (or reject for a legitimate business reason) any order submitted by Distributor.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time frame for the company to accept or reject orders to one business day may be unreasonably short, especially considering the complexities of order processing, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Florida law, where the contract is governed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Burger King Corp. v. Weaver, 169 F.3d 1310 (11th Cir. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract, requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably. Reducing the order acceptance timeframe to one day could be seen as a lack of good faith, particularly if it hinders the distributor's ability to effectively conduct business, potentially violating this implied covenant. This is because such a short timeframe may not allow for adequate review and processing of orders, especially for a company importing from Brazil.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time for the company to accept or reject orders from seven days to one day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both argue that this violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Florida law because one day may not be enough time to adequately review and process orders. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, because the time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements and the deadline violates state law (implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing). The scraped snippets were empty but because the prompt allows me to use the rest of the given information, the legal explanation is sufficient to make a judgement.

perturbation.original_text: Within Seven (7) days of receipt of such Products, Distributor will notify Company of any shortages, defects, non-conformance, and Company will promptly replace such Products free of charge.
perturbation.changed_text: Within Two (2) days of receipt of such Products, Distributor will notify Company of any shortages, defects, non-conformance, and Company will promptly replace such Products free of charge.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification timeframe for shortages, defects, or non-conformance to two days could be considered unreasonably short, conflicting with the implied warranty of merchantability, which requires a reasonable time for inspection under the UCC as adopted by Florida.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.602 - Manner and Effect of Rightful Rejection
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.602 requires the buyer to reject goods within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. Shortening the notification period to two days may not afford a 'reasonable time' for inspection, particularly for imported goods, and could therefore be considered a breach of the buyer's rights under Florida's adoption of the UCC. This statute applies since the contract involves the sale of goods.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 602 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 602 Manner and effect of rightful rejection. F.S. 672.602 672.602 Manner and effect of rightful rejection. — (1) Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. It is ineffective unless the buyer seasonably notifies the seller. (2) Subject to the provisions of the two following sections on rejected goods (ss. 672.603 and 672.604 ): (a) After rejection any exercise of ownership by the buyer with respect to any commercial unit is wrongful as against the seller; and (b) If the buyer has before rejection taken physical possession of goods in which he or she does not have a security interest under the provisions of this chapter (s. 672.711 (3)), the buyer is under a duty after rejection to hold them with reasonable care at the seller’s disposition for a time sufficient to permit the seller to remove them; but (c) The buyer has no further obligations with regard to goods rightfully rejected. (3) The seller’s rights with respect to goods wrongfully rejected are governed by the provisions of this chapter on seller’s remedies in general (s. 672.703 ). History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 587, ch. 97-102. Note. — s. 2-602, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification timeframe to two days, which may not be a 'reasonable time' as required by Florida Statutes § 672.602. The law snippet states: 'Rejection of goods must be within a reasonable time after their delivery or tender. It is ineffective unless the buyer seasonably notifies the seller.' Shortening the period to two days could be deemed unreasonable, contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Recipient Party under this Section 6 shall survive termination or nonrenewal of this Agreement for a period of thee (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Recipient Party under this Section 6 shall survive termination or nonrenewal of this Agreement for a period of one hundred and eighty (180) days.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 180 days (approximately 6 months) may be insufficient to protect the Disclosing Party's proprietary information, especially considering the competitive nature of the market. This could be deemed unconscionable or unreasonable, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable under Florida law.
perturbation.law_citation: Kohl v. Bay Colony Club Condo., Inc., 398 So.2d 865 (Fla. 4th DCA 1981)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Florida law, a contract clause may be deemed unconscionable if it is so outrageous as to shock the conscience. Reducing the confidentiality period for proprietary information to only 180 days, particularly in a distributor agreement where long-term market strategies and customer relationships are involved, could be considered unreasonably short and fail to provide adequate protection, potentially being deemed unconscionable under the established principles in Kohl v. Bay Colony Club Condo., Inc.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to 180 days. The perturbation explanation and law explanation suggest this is an unreasonable amount of time, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable under Florida law, especially considering the competitive nature of the market. Since the contract now implements a term that is argued as unconscionable under established principles, this falls under the definition of legal contradiction as it makes the contract unenforceable. Although no snippets were provided, the change directly undermines the purpose of protecting proprietary information for a reasonable amount of time.
